title: Clean Fuels Weekly Commentary: CA LCFS Amendments Delayed; OAL Disapproval Sparks Uncertainty Over 2025 Implementation
content:
This Weekâ€™s Highlights:

(17th February â€“ 23rd February)
  • CARB faced a setback when the OAL disapproved the amendments to the LCFS on February 18, 2025. This caused an immediate drop in LCFS spot prices by 16.61%, reflecting market uncertainty about potential delays.
  • A total of 680,130 credits were exchanged as Type 1 trades, a fall of 3.10% week-over-week (WoW), and all non-zero trades witnessed a fall of 25.50% WoW as 953,225 credits were traded in CA LCFS.
  • The weekly average CA LCFS credit price decreased this week to $71.12/MTÂ for Type 1Â transfers, recording a 4.10%Â fall WoW; All Non-Zero transfers rose by 5.92% in price, reaching $69.97/MT.
  • The average LCFS spot credit price decreased by 10.94% WoW; on 25th Feb, the spot price stood at $60.50/MT and the benchmark price stood at $64.25/MT.
  • D3 RIN prices increased by 0.81% WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 7.41% WoW.

cCarbonâ€™s Market View

The California Air Resources Board (CARB) faced a setback when the Office of Administrative Law (OAL) disapproved the amendments to the Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) on February 18, 2025, due to technical issues with clarity. This caused an immediate drop in LCFS credit prices by 16.61%, reflecting market uncertainty about potential delays. However, CARB responded swiftly, referencing its past experience with the 2022 Commercial Harbor Craft regulation disapproval, where it revised and resubmitted the rule within three months. Following an updated market notice on February 20, CARB assured the market that the disapproval was based on procedural issues, not on the merits of the regulation, and emphasized that Â "incorrect procedure" was listed as the basis for disapproval. After this reassurance, credit prices slightly recovered, rising 2.4% to $64.00 on February 21. However, by February 24, LCFS credit prices dropped again to $60, and further to $60.50 by February 25, signaling continued market uncertainty. CARB would have 120 days to address OAL's concerns and resubmit the revised regulations, potentially including a 15-day public comment period. If CARBâ€™s revisions and resubmission go smoothly, the regulations could be finalized by mid-2025 or even 2026. However, several factors could delay the process, including CARBâ€™s internal challenges, additional rounds of public comment, and ongoing litigation. Three lawsuits currently challenge CARBâ€™s actions and could influence the rulemaking process. The first, Food & Water Watch et al. v. CARB (Case No. 24CECG05508), focuses on the continued use of avoided methane credits, a case set for hearing on April 11, 2025. The second, Communities for a Better Environment et al. v. CARB (Case No. 24CECG05430), argues that CARB's final Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) violated California's Environmental Quality Act (CEQA), which could necessitate additional environmental reviews. The third, Growth Energy v. CARB (Case No. 24CECG05514), raises concerns about the structure and implementation of the LCFS program itself, potentially leading to significant changes if the court finds merit in its arguments. Market expectations for detailed feedback from the OAL on February 25th have not been met, leaving stakeholders eagerly waiting. Uncertainty hangs over the implementation timeline for the amended LCFS regulations. Market participants are now considering the possibility of delays extending into mid-2025 or even 2026, which could further impact market dynamics and credit prices in the coming months.

CA LCFS Weekly Price and Volume Trend

[caption id="attachment_592785" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 1. Weekly average credit prices and weekly volumes, Source: California ARB[/caption] The average Type 1 prices recorded a 4.10% WoW fall, decreasing from $74.16 to $71.12 this week, with all non-zero credit prices increased by 5.92% WoW, rising from $66.06 to $69.97. Type 1 trades saw 682,130 credits exchanged, reflecting a decline of 3.10% WoW. The total transaction volume for all non-zero trades saw a decrease of 25.50% to reach 953,225 credits.Â 

CA LCFS Daily Average Spot Price

[caption id="attachment_592784" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 2. California LCFS Weekly average spot prices and benchmark (*CC.Info OTC: Numbers are an average of broker prices reported to CC.info)[/caption] Last week, the average LCFS spot credit prices decreased by 10.94% WoW, reaching $64.75/MT. On 25th February, the average spot price was $60.50/MT, while the benchmark, representing the future price of contracts expiring at the end of 2025, stood at $64.25/MT on 25th February.

CA LCFS Weekly Trade Frequency and Volume

[caption id="attachment_592783" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 3. Trade frequency and volume, Source: California ARB[/caption] As per the data reported by CARB last week, 50 trades were reported in the market, with three high-volume trades. The highest transfer by price was $74.00 for a Type 2 transaction at a volume of 23 credits, which was also the smallest transfer volume. Last week, the lowest transfer by price was $46.00 per credit of Type 2, reported for a transaction at volume of 10,000 credits. The largest volume transfers of 500,000 credits of the Type 1 category were undertaken for a price of $72.75.

CA LCFS Monthly Volumes and Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_592782" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 4. Total monthly volumes and associated prices, Source: California ARB[/caption] In February, following the disapproval of the LCFS amendments by the OAL, the market saw significant changes. By February 23, the Type 1 average spot price had decreased to $69.53, reflecting the market's reaction to the OAL's rejection of the November 2024 amendments approved by CARB. Trading volumes for the month stood at 4.61 MMT, recorded up until February 23. While this price drop marked a shift from Januaryâ€™s $72.56, the market remains in a state of uncertainty as it awaits CARBâ€™s next steps in the regulatory process once OAL releases its feedback in detail.

CA LCFS Weekly Volume Price Trend

[caption id="attachment_592781" align="alignnone" width="1000"] Fig 5. Volume Price Trend indicator for the CA LCFS market, Source: California ARB[/caption] The Volume Price Trend Indicator (VPT) helps identify the parity between supply and demand for a stock. The idea behind the indicator is to multiply the market volume of LCFS credits by the percentage change in its price. If the credit price declines, the value of the indicator falls. Across 17th Feb â€“ 23rd Feb, total VPT recorded a 15.08% increase (WoW), with all non-zero prices rising over the previous week.

Renewable Fuel Standard (RFS): RIN weekly price trend

RINs are credits used for compliance and are the â€œcurrencyâ€ of the RFS program. Renewable fuel producers generate RINs, market participants trade RINs, and obligated parties obtain and then ultimately retire RINs for the compliance list. D3 RIN prices increased by 0.81% WoW, D4, D5 and D6 RIN prices increased by 7.41% WoW. D3: Cellulosic biofuel D4: Biomass-based diesel D5: Advanced biofuels (produced from any type of renewable biomass except corn starch ethanol) D6: Conventional renewable biofuel ethanol derived from corn starch, or any other qualifying renewable fuel

Upcoming dates

Key Clean Fuels Market Developments this week

  • Aemetis Ethanol Plant Passes $2 Billion Revenues Milestone; Expects $40 Million Annually From Energy Efficiency Project. Aemetis, Inc. (NASDAQ: AMTX), a renewable natural gas and renewable fuels company operating in the U.S. and India, announced that cumulative revenues generated by the Aemetis ethanol plant in California passed the $2 billion milestone. The Aemetis Keyes 65 million gallon per year capacity ethanol plant has been operating since 2011, delivering more than 768 million gallons of ethanol ($1.6 billion revenues) to the California market.
  • Hydrogen Industry Urges Congress to Preserve Section 45V Tax Credit for Clean Energy Leadership. A coalition of businesses and organizations from the U.S. hydrogen industry is urging congressional leaders to maintain the Section 45V tax credit for clean hydrogen production. They argue that this incentive is crucial for securing U.S. energy dominance, boosting economic growth, and enhancing national security by reducing reliance on foreign energy. The industry, poised to invest billions following delayed regulatory guidance, warns that without the credit, jobs and investments could shift overseas. They call on lawmakers to preserve the tax incentive as they consider major tax legislation in 2025.
  • Clean Fuels, Others Urge EPA Administrator Zeldin to Set Robust, Multiyear RFS Volumes. Clean Fuels Alliance America joined 10 other national trade groups in a letter to EPA Administrator Lee Zeldin urging the agency to set robust, timely, multiyear Renewable Fuel Standards for 2026 and beyond. The groups â€“ which include the American Petroleum Institute â€“ represent fuel producers, marketers, and agricultural feedstock suppliers who agree that strong RFS volumes will help deliver U.S. energy dominance.
  • Information Regarding Low Carbon Fuel Standard Regulation Updates. The California Air Resources Board (CARB) is issuing this notice to inform market participants and the public of the Office of Administrative Lawâ€™s (OAL) decision to disapprove amendments to the Low Carbon Fuel Standard (LCFS) Regulation.1 CARB approved these LCFS amendments for adoption at a November 8, 2024, hearing.
  • Montana Renewables Announces Closing of $1.44 Billion DOE Loan Facility for Renewable Fuels and Biomass Energy Facility. Calumet, Inc. announced the closing of a $1.44 billion guaranteed loan facility with the U.S. Department of Energy (â€œDOEâ€) Loan Programs Office (â€œLPOâ€). The loan will fund the construction and expansion of the renewable fuels facility owned by Montana Renewables, LLC (â€œMontana Renewablesâ€ or â€œMRLâ€), an unrestricted subsidiary of Calumet.
  • 2025 State of the Industry Report â€“ Renewable Fuels Association. Geoff Cooperâ€™s State of the Industry address at the National Ethanol Conference (NEC) reflected on the roots of the ethanol industry and laid out a vision for its future under the new Trump administration. Farm income has declined 24% since 2022, with lower corn prices and higher production costs, surpluses are pushing commodity prices down, making it harder for farmers to remain profitable and regulatory barriers continue to limit ethanolâ€™s full potential, particularly restrictions on E15 fuel sales.
  • Darling Ingredients Inc. Converts Wastewater Emissions Into Renewable Natural Gas, Cutting Scope 1 Emissions. Darling Ingredients Inc., the worldâ€™s leading company turning food waste into sustainable products and producer of renewable energy, announced that it has begun making renewable natural gas (RNG) derived from wastewater at the companyâ€™s Dublin, Georgia, rendering facility, reducing Scope 1 emissions while creating a new revenue stream. In partnership with GreenGasUSA, the RNG is injected into the City of Dublinâ€™s natural gas distribution system and purchased by the Municipal Gas Authority of Georgia and integrated into the existing natural gas energy grid, generating thermal energy credits.
